messages.content: Loftwren | Senior Manager, Pricing and Analytics | applied | 27 days | B+ fit | 76% match | ghost risk high | docs tailored. The posting is off their careers page and the recruiter's mailbox bounces.

applied → closed. Nobody rejected you — the req is off the page and the recruiter's mailbox bounces, so there is no one left to make a decision. You end it. Twenty-seven days at high ghost risk had already told you this. The tailored pricing docs still travel.

Here are all of Yolanda Prieto's resume bullets, numbered:

1. Built the department's first grants calendar and reporting tracker; on-time federal report submission went from 76% to 100%.
2. Administers 46 active federal and state grants totaling $88M for a county health and human services department.
3. Wrote or edited 19 grant applications last cycle with a 63% award rate, above the department's 41% historical rate.
4. Serves as the department's point of contact for two federal project officers.
5. Monitors 23 subrecipients including annual risk assessments and 9 on-site reviews a year.
6. Trained 40 program staff on Uniform Guidance allowability rules.
7. Closed out 14 expired awards with full drawdown and no returned funds.
8. Cut average subrecipient invoice turnaround from 34 days to 12.
9. Recovered $2.3M in previously unbilled reimbursable costs by rebuilding the indirect-cost allocation schedule.
10. Reduced the single-audit finding count from 7 to 0 across two consecutive audit years.

They're applying to a Contracts and Procurement Manager opening at Kingsford County, Washington. The job leads a newly centralized procurement division covering about $180M a year in spend, manages complex solicitations, supervises four buyers and a contracts specialist, and prepares contract award items for the Board of Commissioners. Which bullets carry the most weight for this specific opening, in priority order?

Career-switch board, and I know some of these are reaches. Rank them and be honest about the reaches.
Bellrose Outfitters | Retail Operations Analyst | interview | B+ | 81% | 5 | low | docs yes
Bramble and Rye Hospitality Group | Operations Analytics Analyst | applied | B | 74% | 10 | low | docs yes
Palladia Logistics | Business Analyst, Distribution Operations | researched | B | 70% | 14 | medium | docs no
Wickham Row | Director of E-commerce Operations | sourced | C | 56% | 22 | high | docs no

The interview is the whole board right now. Bellrose Outfitters first: B+, 81%, five days in, docs tailored, and it's the most direct translation of store operations into an analyst seat.
Bramble and Rye Hospitality Group second — applied ten days ago at 74%, low risk, tailored. Wait one more week before nudging.
Palladia Logistics third: 70%, fourteen days researched, nothing written.
Wickham Row fourth, and it's the reach you asked about — a director title at 56% with high ghost risk after 22 days.
Drop Wickham Row. The other three are the actual switch.

I direct last-mile delivery across 22 metro markets — 1,900 daily routes, 6.4M parcels a year — and I cut cost per stop from $4.62 to $3.71 through route densification and a mixed employee/contractor model. I also negotiated the delivery service partner contract template we now use with 64 contracted fleets, and I own a $140M delivery operating budget. There's a Director of Field Operations role at Tallow Creek Market Group in Atlanta that pairs 48 grocery stores with a same-day delivery operation, wants either store labor model or route-level delivery economics experience, and P&L ownership with the ability to defend a variance to a CFO. How do I compare?

The route-level delivery economics are a direct match — your cost-per-stop reduction and the contracted-fleet template cover exactly what the posting asks for on the delivery side, and the $140M budget demonstrates P&L ownership at a scale beyond what defending a CFO variance would require. Gap: nothing here shows store labor model experience, and this role explicitly pairs delivery with 48 physical grocery stores you'd also need to run. Risk: grocery is a different business than parcel delivery, and your background doesn't show retail store operations at all, just the delivery layer. You'd cover half the job well and start from zero on the other half.

Nothing has moved in three weeks and I've stopped looking at it.

role: Director, Ambulatory Operations - Marrow Ridge Health
stage: applied / fit A- / match 85% / 29 days / ghost high / docs yes

role: Operations Manager, Home Infusion - Wexley Care Partners
stage: conversation / fit B+ / match 76% / 23 days / ghost medium / docs yes

role: Clinical Informatics Analyst - Verity Ridge Health Network
stage: researched / fit B / match 67% / 34 days / ghost low / docs no

role: Associate Medical Writer - Osterby Clinical Communications
stage: sourced / fit C / match 40% / 41 days / ghost medium / docs no

Wexley is the recoverable one. Twenty-three days in conversation, but only medium ghost risk and the docs are ready. Write to your contact today and apply rather than waiting for them to restart it.
Marrow Ridge at 29 days applied and high ghost risk gets one closing note. If A- and 85% produced no reply in a month, it needs a person, not another portal check.
Verity Ridge has sat in researched 34 days with no docs. Write them or drop it, because 67% doesn't survive half-effort.
Osterby, C at 40% after 41 days: gone.

Second closing for the same Quillbase Director of Product, Automation letter. The posting mentions running the customer advisory session on automation each quarter as a responsibility. Write a different 1-2 sentence closing with a specific ask about that.
How the quarterly automation customer advisory session runs today, and what you'd want a new director to bring to it, is something I'm keen to hear about. Happy to find a time this week.

Applying to Vermilion Trail Journeys for Operations Manager, Guided Travel. JD wants a per-passenger land cost built and defended line by line. My current bullet: "Cut per-passenger land cost 12% by renegotiating 31 supplier contracts and consolidating three ground handlers into one." Make it land better for this one.

Cut per-passenger land cost 12% by renegotiating 31 supplier contracts and consolidating three ground handlers into one — land cost built and defended line by line, exactly what this operations manager role wants.

Three replies this week. Rank everything before I get overconfident.

· Kettlebridge Depot · WMS Solutions Analyst · interview · A- · 86% · 3 days · low · docs yes
· Tarnwell Logistics Group · Senior Manager, Fulfillment Systems · conversation · A · 89% · 6 days · low · docs yes
· Steadmoor Distribution · Continuous Improvement Analyst, Network Operations · applied · B+ · 78% · 9 days · low · docs yes
· Palladia Logistics · Business Analyst, Distribution Operations · applied · B · 72% · 16 days · medium · docs yes
· Drayline Distribution · Transportation Manager, Outbound · researched · C · 61% · 12 days · medium · docs no

Overconfidence would be treating all five as equal. Kettlebridge Depot is first: interview, three days, A-, 86%, docs tailored — the only role that can produce an offer this month. Tarnwell Logistics Group second at A, 89%, the strongest fit and a Senior Manager step up, but a six-day conversation is two stages behind Kettlebridge. Steadmoor Distribution third, B+, 78%, nine days at applied, low risk. Palladia Logistics fourth — B, 72%, sixteen days, medium risk, one follow-up left in it. Drayline Distribution fifth and dropped: C, 61%, no docs, and the only role here that is not systems or network analysis.

Trying to punch up this bullet for a Grants Manager application at Foothill Community Health Alliance: "Cut average subrecipient invoice turnaround from 34 days to 12." They want someone who reconciles grant revenue and expenditure monthly with the controller. Give me a stronger version.
Cut average subrecipient invoice turnaround from 34 days to 12 — the financial reconciliation discipline this grants manager role will run jointly with the controller every month.

Trying to punch up this bullet for a Senior Manager, Pricing and Analytics application at Loftwren: "Cut manual rate-loading time from 6 hours per week to 40 minutes by scripting the extranet upload in Python." Their JD leans hard on hands-on Python analysis. Give me a stronger version.
Scripted the extranet rate-loading workflow in Python, cutting a 6-hour weekly task to 40 minutes — proof this pricing logic won't wait around for a manual upload.
High ghost risk on almost everything. How do I tell which postings are real before I spend an hour tailoring a resume?

Check three things in about two minutes each. First, open the posting from the card and see whether the employer's own careers page still lists it; aggregator copies outlive the original constantly. Second, look for a posted or refreshed date, and treat anything older than eight weeks with no update as low priority. Third, check whether the same title has been posted repeatedly this year, which usually means a comp or bar problem rather than a fake role. Order your board by that assessment and tailor only for the top of it. Two searches a day is enough once you are filtering this way.
Too few results. Production manager, Cedar Rapids. Titles 'production manager food'. Keywords 'SMED, OEE, SQF, tier board, autonomous maintenance'. Locations 'Cedar Rapids'.

A qualified title and five continuous improvement terms over-filter on both axes at once. Titles become 'production manager, plant manager, operations manager, continuous improvement manager'. Keep 'SQF, OEE' and drop 'SMED, tier board, autonomous maintenance'. Add 'Iowa City, Marion, Waterloo, Dubuque' to locations. Two runs a day is enough now, and the remaining eight are not worth burning. Food and packaging plants in eastern Iowa fill roles through plant-manager networks and their own websites long before an aggregator lists them, so pick twelve within an hour's drive, check them monthly, and add openings with Add role.

Write a leadership-focused resume summary for Imani Sørlie using only these facts: she facilitated a 14-member stakeholder working group of local governments, developers and tenant advocates to consensus on a formula change; she trains new analysts on her agency's methodology standards and peer-review process; she has presented findings to the agency's oversight board 7 times in two years.

Imani Sørlie facilitated a 14-member stakeholder working group of local governments, developers and tenant advocates to consensus on a formula change. She trains new analysts on her agency's methodology standards and peer-review process, and has presented findings to the agency's oversight board 7 times in two years.

Weekly board check, everything is moving for once.

Northline Coach Company:
  title: Manager, Commercial Network Planning
  stage: interview · fit: A- · match: 88% · days: 3 · ghost: low · docs: yes
Windrose Air Group:
  title: Manager, Network Planning
  stage: applied · fit: A · match: 90% · days: 7 · ghost: low · docs: yes
Wanderfare Group:
  title: Senior Manager, Revenue Science
  stage: conversation · fit: B+ · match: 80% · days: 5 · ghost: low · docs: no
Loftwren:
  title: Senior Manager, Pricing and Analytics
  stage: researched · fit: B · match: 72% · days: 9 · ghost: medium · docs: no

Healthy board — this is sequencing, not triage.
1. Northline Coach Company. Interview, three days, 88%, docs prepared; the only role with a date attached.
2. Windrose Air Group — highest fit here at A and 90%, applied a week, low risk. Second only because nobody has scheduled you yet.
3. Wanderfare Group, B+ 80%, conversation five days, docs outstanding. Tiebreak over Loftwren: the live conversation beat a researched role.
4. Loftwren, B 72%, nine days, medium risk.
Nothing gets dropped. No role here is over nine days old or above medium ghost risk — subtracting now would be premature.

Extract company, title, seniority, location, and requirements from this JD as raw JSON. Nothing else in your reply.

Careers / All Jobs / Search Results

Junipero Parcel is hiring: Vice President, Last-Mile Delivery
Location: Philadelphia, PA — Hybrid, 3 days on-site
Level: Vice president

Requirements: Owned a nine-figure operating budget with accountability for cost per stop or equivalent unit economics. Peak season planning at volumes that double or more. You have expanded a delivery network into new metros and know what breakeven density actually costs.

This description is a summary and may not include every duty.

{"company": "Junipero Parcel", "title": "Vice President, Last-Mile Delivery", "seniority": "Vice president", "location": "Philadelphia, PA — Hybrid, 3 days on-site", "requirements": ["Owned a nine-figure operating budget with accountability for cost per stop or equivalent unit economics", "Peak season planning at volumes that double or more", "You have expanded a delivery network into new metros and know what breakeven density actually costs"]}

I'm applying to Marrow Ridge Health for the Director, Ambulatory Operations role. The posting requires survey experience, Joint Commission or CMS, with the paperwork trail to prove it. Write a 1-2 sentence cover letter opener hooking on that specific requirement.

Survey readiness that holds up under Joint Commission or CMS scrutiny takes more than good intentions, it takes a documented trail. That's the discipline I want to bring to Marrow Ridge's outpatient division.

I'm looking at a Senior Manager, Revenue Science role at a travel marketplace covering flights, hotels, and packaged trips — about nine million annual bookings. It comes with a team of three analysts, growing to five, and the person owns the demand forecast used for commercial planning. My background is hotel revenue management: I trained 9 property-level revenue analysts on a new rate-shopping workflow and all 9 passed certification, I migrated our demand model to a Snowflake and Tableau stack cutting refresh time from overnight to 11 minutes, and I piloted dynamic group pricing at four resorts, converting 22 additional group blocks. How do I stack up on the people-management side?

Reasonably well, with a caveat. Training 9 analysts to certification and running a group-pricing pilot show you can teach a workflow and get people to adopt it, which is close to what leading a small growing analyst team requires. The Snowflake and Tableau migration is a good technical credential for a role that partners with data science. The caveat: training analysts on a defined workflow is not the same as owning a team's output and growth as direct reports — you haven't managed people with that reporting-line accountability. Risk: they may test whether you can set priorities for three analysts working on ambiguous forecasting problems, not just certify them on a known process.

Here's my resume summary and a job I found. Production Manager, food-grade packaging line, 3 shifts, 96 operators, running 24/5. Led the tier-board daily management rollout giving each shift a 10-minute structured start; cut minor stoppages on two bottleneck fillers 44% via an operator-owned autonomous maintenance routine; trained and certified 22 operators to multi-skill level 3, doubling coverage flexibility; managed commissioning of a new $4.8M case packer from FAT to steady-state in 6 weeks. The role: Continuous Improvement Manager, Brightloom Foods, Cedar Rapids, on-site, building the annual CI plan and tying projects to the plant P&L, facilitating kaizen across four production areas.

Good match: your tier-board rollout and operator-owned autonomous maintenance work are exactly the daily-management foundation this role asks for, and your equipment commissioning experience adds credibility to a plant that's been investing in capital. Facilitating kaizen across four production areas builds directly on training 22 operators to multi-skill status. One open item: your background shows tactical shop-floor projects but not experience building an annual CI plan or tying results explicitly to the plant P&L - confirm you can operate at that planning level before applying. Another shift: you'd move from owning shift output directly to reporting savings and attainment monthly, a step removed from the floor.

I'm overloaded and the travel season starts Monday. Rank, please.
Vermilion Trail Journeys | Operations Manager, Guided Travel | sourced | A | 90% | 2 days | low | no
Beacon Halloway Events | Supplier Performance Manager | interview | C | 64% | 5 days | medium | yes
Saltmoor Expeditions | Director of Global Operations | applied | B+ | 80% | 11 days | low | yes
Windward Voyages | Vice President, Commercial | researched | B | 70% | 20 days | high | no
Northline Coach Company | Manager, Commercial Network Planning | applied | A- | 86% | 6 days | low | yes

Beacon Halloway Events leads despite the C and 64% — it's the only interview on the board and it's five days old. A live process outranks a better paper fit every time.
Northline Coach Company second: A-/86%, six days applied, docs in, low risk.
Saltmoor Expeditions third — B+/80%, 11 days, same low risk, just older.
Vermilion Trail Journeys fourth. A/90% is the best fit here, but it's still sourced; it needs work you don't have before Monday.
Windward Voyages fifth.
Drop Windward Voyages: 20 days researched, B/70%, high ghost risk, no docs. Carry four into the season.

Cinderpeak Resorts is hiring a Senior Vice President, Revenue and Distribution. Requirements include fifteen years in commercial or revenue leadership, multi-property revenue management with accountability for total revenue, distribution economics across brand.com, OTA, wholesale and group, ancillary and on-property revenue programs, and having led a pricing or inventory system replacement. Here's my full skills list: commercial strategy, cruise revenue management, net yield optimization, itinerary deployment planning, distribution channel strategy, direct-to-consumer growth, trade partner management, pricing architecture, onboard revenue, marketing P&L, demand forecasting, contact center operations, executive presentation, organizational leadership, capital investment cases, loyalty program strategy, Salesforce, Power BI. Which should I include?

["commercial strategy", "cruise revenue management", "net yield optimization", "distribution channel strategy", "direct-to-consumer growth", "trade partner management", "pricing architecture", "onboard revenue", "demand forecasting", "executive presentation", "organizational leadership"]
Does editing the job description text save automatically, or do I need to click something?

It autosaves — there's no save button for the JD box. Stop typing for about 800 milliseconds and CareerOps writes the text to the card; the hint then reads "· saved to the card ✓." That applies whether the JD arrived by auto-load, was pasted with no link, or is being edited afterward.

Vice President, Customer Success at Tessellate Software (Boston, MA). Bullets:
1. Leads a 31-person customer success organization covering $74M in annual recurring revenue.
2. Raised gross revenue retention from 87% to 94% in two years across a 900-account book.
3. Built the health-scoring model that now predicts churn 90 days out with 71% precision.
4. Cut time-to-value for new enterprise accounts from 74 days to 38 through a redesigned onboarding program.
5. Grew expansion revenue from $4.1M to $12.6M by giving CSMs a structured account-planning motion and a quota.
6. Restructured the team into segment pods, which lifted CSM capacity from 22 to 34 accounts each without a satisfaction drop.
7. Reduced CSM attrition from 29% to 12% after rebuilding the career ladder and compensation plan.
8. Partnered with product to launch an in-app adoption program that moved a key feature from 19% to 58% usage.
9. Runs quarterly business reviews for the top 40 accounts personally.
10. Owns a $3.9M departmental budget and the customer success technology stack.
11. Cut the escalation queue from an average of 41 open items to 9 by introducing a defined severity framework with product.

Needs: led a customer success organization of 25 people or more. Role: rebuild health scoring so leading indicators arrive early enough to act on.

Given this JD, which bullets carry the most weight? List them in priority order.

Saltmoor Expeditions again, Director of Global Operations, but the conversation track - their COO called 9 days ago and asked me for references. Stage conversation, A-, 82%, ghost risk low, docs ready. I've been sitting on it.

He asked for references nine days ago - send them. That is the follow-up. No commentary beyond a line naming each person and why they're relevant to global operations. Doing the thing that was asked, on the same thread, restarts the process without any nudge at all. Get it out today; a reference request left for two weeks reads as disorganized.

All my bullets are numbered below, followed by the opening I'm targeting. Order the most relevant ones first so I know what to lead with.

1. Negotiated a supply contract change on infusion consumables saving $126K a year without a formulary change.
2. Manages 38 staff across two ambulatory infusion centers serving roughly 1,900 patients a month.
3. Wrote the sepsis-screening escalation protocol now used across four sites, cutting time-to-antibiotic to 44 minutes.
4. Built the productivity dashboard that leadership uses for monthly staffing decisions across 11 clinics.
5. Led the Epic scheduling-template rebuild for oncology infusion, which removed 1,200 annual double-booked slots.
6. Precepted 14 new graduate nurses through the extended orientation pathway; 13 stayed past 18 months.
7. Shortened average chair-turnover time from 47 minutes to 31 minutes by resequencing pharmacy prep and nurse assignment.
8. Chairs the site safety committee; closed 87% of reported near-miss events within 14 days.
9. Coordinated the COVID-era conversion of a procedure suite into overflow infusion capacity in 9 days.
10. Held nurse turnover at 11% during a year when the system average was 23%.
11. Passed two unannounced Joint Commission surveys with zero condition-level findings.

Wexley Care Partners delivers infusion therapy in patients' homes across Ohio and Kentucky with about 2,600 active patients and 70 field nurses. They are hiring an Operations Manager, Home Infusion who has five years in healthcare operations with at least two managing clinical or field staff, experience scheduling and planning capacity for a mobile or dispersed workforce, a track record designing or rebuilding a workflow that crosses pharmacy, clinical, and intake teams, and comfort owning productivity and quality dashboards. Nurse retention is called out as the single biggest cost driver they want addressed.

Write a general resume summary for Wren Delacroix, targeting healthcare data analyst roles, leading with her leadership and training experience. Use these facts: she trained 9 new technologists on the middleware autoverification rules, validated 4 new assays including method comparison, precision and reportable-range studies, and presented a quality-improvement poster on hemolysis reduction at a state laboratory conference.

Trained 9 new technologists on middleware autoverification rules and validated 4 new assays, including method comparison, precision and reportable-range studies. Presented a quality-improvement poster on hemolysis reduction at a state laboratory conference. Comfortable teaching technical material and defending it in front of a room of peers.

I'm tailoring my resume for the Manager, Research and Evaluation opening at Ridgeline Policy Collaborative. This person carries a portfolio of three to five concurrent engagements. Here's my bullet: "Analyzes housing production and affordability policy for a state agency administering $1.4B in annual program funding." Sharpen it for this JD.
Housing and affordability policy work behind $1.4B in annual program funding is the scale of engagement this research and evaluation manager role would carry across three to five concurrent projects.

I led my store through a full remodel with 11 trading days lost against a plan of 18, and I reduced out-of-stock rate on our top 200 SKUs from 6.8% to 2.9% by changing the replenishment review cadence. I run a 62-employee store doing $19M in annual sales, top quartile in a 140-store region. Bellrose Outfitters has a Retail Operations Analyst opening that wants someone who can explain a labor model to a store manager without using the word 'model,' plus experience diagnosing underperforming locations and replacing legacy spreadsheets with governed reporting. How does my operations background translate to that analyst seat?

Running a top-quartile store at $19M with 62 employees gives you real standing to diagnose underperforming locations — you've been the store manager on the other side of that conversation. The replenishment cadence change behind the OOS drop from 6.8% to 2.9% is a concrete, transferable example of exactly the kind of fix this role is meant to scale across other stores. The remodel result, 11 days lost against an 18-day plan, shows execution discipline but isn't directly about reporting. Nothing here demonstrates you've built governed reporting to replace ad hoc spreadsheets, which is a named responsibility, not just a nice-to-have.

Emberline Health, Clinical Content Lead. Good call with their clinical director; she said the formal posting requires an application from everyone and pointed me at the req. conversation, 11 days, B+ fit, 79%, ghost risk low, docs yes.

conversation → applied. When the director points at the req, the conversation has done its job - eleven days converted into a warm referral and your docs are ready. Apply within forty-eight hours and tell her it is in, while she still remembers the call.

Grade this candidate against this role. Return ONLY JSON, no other text: {"score":int,"summary":"...","strengths":[...],"gaps":[...]}

ROLE: Steadmoor Distribution - Continuous Improvement Analyst, Network Operations (Senior individual contributor; parcel carrier, nine cross-dock facilities and a contracted delivery fleet)
NEEDS: four or more years in logistics or supply chain analytics; SQL and a BI tool, Power BI; process improvement method, root cause discipline or DMAIC; warehouse throughput or route-level delivery economics; be in facilities rather than modelling from a desk; findings become standard work for other sites; Green Belt a plus
CANDIDATE: Jared Whitlock - Store Operations Lead, Charlotte, NC.
- Taught himself SQL; built a Tableau dashboard covering 11 stores
- Wrote the district's holiday staffing playbook, used by 11 stores
- Spends his time in the operation, not behind a desk
SKILLS: SQL, Tableau, process documentation

{"score": 38, "summary": "His operational analysis instincts are good and the setting is wrong. Warehouse throughput and route economics are both new, and the role sits above his level.", "strengths": ["SQL and dashboard building in Tableau are established; Power BI is a tool change, not a skill change", "He spends time in the operation rather than modelling it from a desk, which the posting values", "Process documentation is a listed skill, and his holiday staffing playbook is used by 11 stores"], "gaps": ["No logistics operations experience; nine cross-dock facilities and a contracted delivery fleet are outside his work", "Neither warehouse throughput nor route-level delivery economics is in his record", "No formal process improvement method: DMAIC, root cause discipline, or a Green Belt", "Four or more years in logistics or supply chain analytics is not met", "A senior individual contributor setting standard work for other sites is above his scope"]}

Blue Heron Health Alliance, Chief Nursing Officer. Applied 25 days, A- fit, 85% match, ghost risk medium, docs tailored. The search firm called this afternoon to set up a first conversation with the panel.

Panel scheduling from the search firm moves this: applied → interview. Twenty-five days at medium risk was starting to look like silence, and this closes that question. Go in ready to speak to system-level nursing scope, which is what the 85% match rests on.

For the Marrow Ridge Health Director, Ambulatory Operations posting, write a 1-2 sentence cover letter closing. The posting mentions leading accreditation and survey readiness for outpatient locations. Ask specifically to discuss that.
Leading accreditation and survey readiness across the outpatient locations is the piece I'd most like to dig into. When could we talk?
Nothing is moving. 17 cards in Sourced, no applications. Clinical lab scientist in Madison targeting healthcare analytics. I keep telling myself I need one more certification first.

The certificate is not the blocker, the application count is. Two searches a day, and pick three roles this week to apply to properly. For each, get a match report first so you know which gaps to address in the resume and which to leave for the interview. Close anything in Sourced that wants five years as an analyst, because that is not the door that opens for you. The door that does open is internal and adjacent: your own health system's analytics team, and the systems nearby, where your lab and Epic experience is the reason to hire you rather than a gap.

I'm tailoring my resume for the Quality Engineer, Machining opening at Kestrel Precision Components. The posting wants someone who assembles and submits PPAP packages for new part numbers. Here's my bullet: "Wrote the PPAP packages for 14 new part numbers, all approved on first submission." Sharpen it for this JD.
Wrote PPAP packages for 14 new part numbers, all approved on first submission — the PPAP assembly and submission track record this quality engineer role owns for every new part.

Grade this candidate against this role. Return ONLY JSON, no other text: {"score":int,"summary":"...","strengths":[...],"gaps":[...]}

ROLE: Marchand Outfitters - VP, Retail Operations (Vice president; Denver, CO hybrid)
NEEDS: multi-unit retail leadership across 100+ locations with a district or regional structure beneath you; a store operating expense budget in the tens of millions; designed or replaced a labour model and lived through the rollout; quantifiable shrink reduction; closing and consolidating stores; a succession pipeline that fills openings internally; analytics instincts - field leaders run on a daily scorecard and expect you to interrogate it
CANDIDATE: Beatriz Nunes - E-commerce Merchandising Manager, Los Angeles, CA. Digital only, no stores.
- Manages 4 merchandisers and a content coordinator
- Partners with marketing, planning and creative on the seasonal launch calendar
- Strong analytics: 46 A/B tests, conversion and markdown analysis, assortment scorecard
SKILLS: e-commerce merchandising, assortment planning, A/B testing, SQL (basic), Excel, Tableau

{"score": 16, "summary": "Digital merchandising against a VP of Retail Operations role. The scope, the function, and the field structure are all different.", "strengths": ["She manages a team of five and partners across planning, marketing, and creative, so cross-functional rhythm is familiar", "Analytics instincts are strong, which the posting names for interrogating a daily scorecard"], "gaps": ["No multi-unit retail leadership; this requires 100-plus locations with a district or regional structure beneath her", "No store operating expense budget in the tens of millions", "No labor model design or rollout", "No shrink reduction results", "No store closures, consolidations, or succession pipeline work"]}

quick one — I open this board every morning and do nothing. break the tie for me.

Halvard & Pike / Senior Manager, Digital Merchandising / interview / A / 92% / 2 days / low / docs yes
Wickham Row / Director of E-commerce Operations / conversation / A- / 83% / 8 days / low / docs no
Northmarch Consumer Brands / Category Manager, Marketplace / applied / B+ / 75% / 24 days / medium / docs yes
Bellrose Outfitters / Retail Operations Analyst / sourced / C / 49% / 30 days / high / docs no

Two days after a Halvard & Pike interview at A/92% — that's your whole morning. Write the follow-up while the conversation is still fresh in your head.
Wickham Row is the step up and it has no tailored docs after eight days of conversation. Build them before that thread cools.
Northmarch: 24 days applied, medium ghost risk. One recruiter note, then stop checking it daily.
Bellrose is a C at 49% and thirty days untouched. That role is why you freeze when you open the board. Delete it.

Pasting a JD below. Respond with just the JSON — keys should be company, title, seniority, location, requirements. No markdown fences, no commentary.

Pass this along: Email | LinkedIn | X

Extract company, title, seniority, location, and requirements from this JD as raw JSON. Nothing else in your reply.

This posting will remain open until the position is filled.

Home > Careers > Open Roles

Verdigris Care Network is hiring: Senior Director, Quality and Regulatory Affairs
Location: Baltimore, MD — Hybrid, 3 days on-site
Level: Senior manager

Qualifications:
- Survey and accreditation experience across more than one regulatory body — CMS, Joint Commission, or state agencies
- Strong facilitation skills; you will chair committees composed of people who do not report to you
- Policy authorship for a multi-site organisation
- Data literacy sufficient to challenge a quality dashboard's construction, not only its results

Twenty-eight days sourced, 55%, high ghost risk: reject Sunderly so you stop counting six roles when you have three.

Small board and I'm second-guessing the whole switch. Rank what I have.
Bellrose Outfitters — Retail Operations Analyst — offer — B — 72% — 3 days — low — docs yes
Bramble and Rye Hospitality Group — Operations Analytics Analyst — interview — A- — 88% — 6 days — medium — docs yes
Palladia Logistics — Business Analyst, Distribution Operations — researched — B+ — 78% — 9 days — low — docs no

You have an offer three days old — that's not a failing switch, that's a switch working.
Bellrose Outfitters ranks first because the offer has a deadline and B at 72% is still the analyst title you wanted.
Bramble and Rye Hospitality Group is a close second and deserves a call today: A- at 88%, six days into interviews, medium ghost risk. Mention the offer and ask for a timeline.
Palladia Logistics third — B+, 78%, researched nine days, docs unwritten.
Nothing gets dropped. With an offer live, Palladia Logistics is your fallback if the Bramble timeline slips.

Junipero Parcel | Vice President, Last-Mile Delivery | conversation | A- | 86% | a board member introduced me to their COO 9 days ago and the COO replied warmly but vaguely | ghost risk medium | tailored docs yes

A warm but vague reply is an invitation you haven't accepted yet. Don't wait for the COO to propose something — propose it. Tuesday: "Would a thirty-minute call in the next two weeks be useful? Happy to work around your calendar." Naming a length and a window makes yes easy. Nine days is exactly when a vague thread either converts or dies, so make the ask now.

Palladia is three days old and a B at 68%. Research it after Bramble is submitted, not alongside.
Bellrose is five days applied at A- and 82% with low risk, and that one's working.
Steadmoor at C and 51% after two idle weeks is the one to cut if anything goes.

My background: I rebuilt our direct booking channel from 21% to 44% of bookings, cutting distribution cost $19M a year, and I cut discount depth from 31% off brochure to 18% while holding occupancy above 97%. I restructured our trade partner program across 1,800 agencies. I'm looking at an SVP Revenue and Distribution role overseeing fourteen resort properties and a vacation-ownership business, setting channel mix and cost of distribution as a reported metric. Fit?

Your direct-channel work and trade partner restructuring translate directly to their channel-mix and distribution-cost mandate, and the discount-depth reduction shows you can hold pricing discipline while protecting occupancy, exactly what they're asking for across the portfolio. The gap is the vacation-ownership piece: nothing in your background touches timeshare or fractional inventory, which this role explicitly folds in as a complication. You're also coming from single-product cruise pricing rather than multi-property resort revenue management, so expect questions on how you'd generalize distribution economics across fourteen physical properties instead of a fleet.

I clicked Add to board but nothing happened — what's wrong?

If company or job title is blank, the modal shows "Company and job title are required." and won't save. Fill in both fields — the posting link is optional — then click "Add to board" again. Once it saves, the button briefly reads "Adding…" and the board reloads with the new role's panel open.

Assessing fit for a CIO role. I'm Director of IT for a county government, Spokane, WA. I delivered the ERP replacement for finance, HR, and payroll for 3,200 staff on schedule and $1.9M under a $22M budget, stood up the public open-data portal now serving 90 datasets and about 12,000 monthly users, and negotiated a $2.4M five-year savings on network and telephony. Skills include ERP implementation, cybersecurity governance under CIS and NIST CSF, public procurement, and capital budgeting. The job: CIO at Three Rivers Regional Authority, Tacoma - a special-purpose body running water, wastewater, and solid waste for eleven member jurisdictions, needing someone to rationalize the application portfolio and testify publicly.

Good alignment: your ERP delivery under budget and your NIST CSF/CIS governance background match their major-system-ownership and cybersecurity requirements closely, and reducing systems from 310 to 214 shows the application rationalization skill they specifically want. Public procurement experience also fits their contract negotiation need. Gap: the Authority runs SCADA and utility control environments alongside the business network, which your county IT background doesn't mention. Also worth noting: testifying before an elected board differs from an open public meeting with hostile questioning, which the JD specifically flags - be ready to speak to that distinction.
